macro_segments.title: Introduction to Perek Five: Payment for Work with Prohibited Wine and the Principle of Being Hired for Forbidden Benefit

micro_segments.title: Overview of the fifth perek: shift from absorption of wine to payment for work with yayin nesech
Difference between last perek (how wine becomes stam yeinam through touch) and this perek (payment for work with yayin nesech)
Introduction to the principle of משתכר באיסורי הנאה (mishtaker be'issurei hana'ah): being hired to work with forbidden items

macro_segments.title: The Dispute: Can One Be Paid Salary for Work with Yayin Nesech? Distinguishing Issurei Hana'ah from Tofes et Damav

micro_segments.title: Gemara's question: Is the prohibition on payment for yayin nesech based on the general principle of issurei hana'ah?
Explanation of issurei hana'ah: example with basar bechalav as a prohibition on benefit but not on payment for service
Distinction between issurei hana'ah and tofes et damav: how status transfers to money only in certain prohibitions
Conclusion: the prohibition on payment for yayin nesech is a special chumra of avodah zarah, not derived from general principles
Practical application: whether one can work in a liquor store selling non-kosher wine

macro_segments.title: The Case of Shvi'is Produce and the Principle of Tofes et Damav: When Status Transfers to Money

micro_segments.title: Story of Rav Yanai's workers who borrowed shvi'is produce and repaid with non-shvi'is produce after the Sabbatical year
Rabbi Yochanan's ruling: the fruit repaid later does not acquire the status of shvi'is because it is not an exchange at the same time
Analogy to etnan (payment to a prostitute): the principle that status only transfers when both objects exist simultaneously

macro_segments.title: The Etnan Sugya: When Payment to a Prostitute Becomes Forbidden and the Requirement of Simultaneous Possession

micro_segments.title: Braisa: if payment is given before or after the act without the object being present, it is not considered etnan and is permitted
Gemara's logical challenge: how can payment be given if no act has occurred, and vice versa?
Resolution: the braisa must mean payment given first and then act rendered, or act rendered first and then payment given
Rabbi Yochanan's strict ruling: etnan status only applies if payment and act are simultaneous, not if one follows the other
Question of Rav Hoshaia: if the woman sanctifies the object before the act, does that constitute taking possession?

macro_segments.title: Defending Rabbi Yochanan Against Contradictory Braisa: The Case of Delayed Payment and the Concept of Apoteke

micro_segments.title: Contradictory braisa: if intercourse occurs first and payment is made even three years later, it is still considered etnan and forbidden
Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak's resolution: distinguishing the case where the object is identified at the time of the act versus after
Application to non-Jewish prostitute: she acquires ownership through payment of services, so possession transfers at the moment of the act
The apoteke case: the woman had property rights (lien) on the specific object at the time of the act, even though physical payment occurred later

macro_segments.title: The Scenario of Paying for Workers' Food and Meals: When Does Payment Make the Giver Responsible for the Food's Prohibition?

micro_segments.title: Rav Sheshet's question: if an employer gives money to workers to buy lunch, is he liable for what they buy?
Three types of prohibited foods workers might buy: shvi'is, terumos u'ma'asros, and yayin nesech
Rashi's interpretation: the concern is that the employer is feeding workers prohibited food, not merely enabling them to buy it
Distinction: if employer gives money upfront with no obligation to pay (separate transaction), workers' choices are their own responsibility
If employer says 'go eat and I will pay the bill': the employer is now directly paying for food, making him responsible for its status

macro_segments.title: The Storekeeper with Running Tab: When Does an Obligation to Pay Make the Employer Responsible for Prohibited Food?

micro_segments.title: Rav Chisda's distinction: if the storekeeper has a regular relationship with the employer (machkifo), payment obligation exists immediately
If there is no running tab relationship: the transaction is separate and the bill comes later, so employer is not feeding them the food
Raba's resolution: it does not matter whether there is a running tab—the key is whether the money was pre-identified or the object was pre-identified
The principle: if a specific amount of money was not pre-designated, the payment is merely a later outstanding debt and does not implicate the employer

macro_segments.title: The Storekeeper with Running Tab: When Does an Obligation to Pay Make the Employer Responsible for Prohibited Food? (continued)

micro_segments.title: The problem arises only if the employer pre-paid the storekeeper: then the food is directly transferred from money the employer provided
Textual reconciliation: the braisa must be discussing the case where the employer pre-paid or directly handed over the food

macro_segments.title: Practical Implications for the Oranges Case and the Concept of Outstanding Debt versus Direct Transaction

micro_segments.title: How the ruling on payment timing applies to the original shvi'is oranges case: repayment after the year is an outstanding debt, not a direct exchange
Broader principle: a transaction becomes direct responsibility only if money or identification occurs at the time the prohibited item is transferred

macro_segments.title: Final Case: Hiring Someone to Break Barrels of Yayin Nesech and the Question of Mishtaker Be'issurei Hana'ah

micro_segments.title: Question: if someone is hired to break empty barrels that once contained yayin nesech, is this considered being paid for work with a prohibited item?
Rav Nachman's ruling: breaking barrels is part of the destruction process, so the worker is blessed, not liable to mishtaker be'issurei hana'ah
